content.text:
<recommended_plugins>
Here is a list of plugins that are available but not installed. If the user's query would benefit from one of these plugins, use the `request_plugin_install` tool to suggest that they install it. Pass the parenthesized ID as `plugin_id`. For example, suggest the Google Drive plugin if the query could possibly be better answered with access to Google Drive.

- Atlassian Rovo (atlassian-rovo@openai-curated-remote)
- Box (box@openai-curated-remote)
- Figma (figma@openai-curated-remote)
- Gmail (gmail@openai-curated-remote)
- Google Calendar (google-calendar@openai-curated-remote)
- Google Drive (google-drive@openai-curated-remote)
- Notion (notion@openai-curated-remote)
- Outlook Calendar (outlook-calendar@openai-curated-remote)
- Outlook Email (outlook-email@openai-curated-remote)
- SharePoint (sharepoint@openai-curated-remote)
- Slack (slack@openai-curated-remote)
- Teams (teams@openai-curated-remote)
</recommended_plugins>

Thoroughly and carefully explore and research the current implementation of this application on this branch. Compare it to the mainline implementation 
Review this implementation and design compared with oauth specifications and relevant authoritative sources
Conduct an adversarial analysis of this implementation

I’ll assess the branch as a security-and-design review against `main`, trace the OAuth-relevant trust boundaries, and validate the conclusions against authoritative specifications. I’m using the security diff-review workflow because this is explicitly a mainline comparison with adversarial analysis.

content.text: The scan preflight cannot yet run because the default Python lacks both Python 3.11’s `tomllib` and its compatibility dependency. I’m locating the workspace’s supported interpreter before proceeding; no repository state has changed.

const patch = "*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /var/folders/hs/pt2n3nn16k3935lw_ljnl5t40000gn/T/codex-security-scans/cyspbot/threat_model.md
+# cyspbot repository threat model
+
+## Overview
+
+cyspbot is a hosted Security Token Service running as two Cloudflare Workers. Its token-exchange worker accepts a subject OIDC token from a configured workload-identity provider and, only after explicit token-policy authorization, obtains a short-lived GitHub App installation token for one repository. Its webhook-receiver worker accepts signed GitHub App webhook deliveries and currently acknowledges them without downstream side effects. Shared packages implement OIDC verification, GitHub App JWT authentication and API calls, request-size handling, and HTTP error responses.
+
+The security-critical asset is not application data: it is the ability to mint a GitHub App installation token. The GitHub App private key and webhook secret are Cloudflare secret bindings; the installation's repository/permission configuration is the upper authority, while the checked-in policy is intended to narrow that authority for individual callers. The service is deliberately a public token endpoint, so it must remain safe when every HTTP parameter, request header, body, and submitted token is hostile.
+
+## Threat Model, Trust Boundaries, and Assumptions
+
+### Assets and objectives
+
+- The GitHub App private key, GitHub App JWTs, installation access tokens, and webhook secret must never be returned, logged, or accepted from an untrusted party.
+- No caller may obtain an installation token unless a signature-verified token from a configured issuer, its issuer-specific binding checks, the canonical requested repository, exact permissions, and a checked-in allow rule all match.
+- Issued tokens must be limited to one repository and the approved GitHub App permissions, and the response must not be cached.
+- Webhook handling must not treat headers or JSON as authority until it verifies the GitHub HMAC and confirms the configured GitHub App target.
+- Denials and upstream failures must not disclose tokens, key material, full subject assertions, or policy contents beyond an operator-safe level.
+
+### Trust boundaries
+
+1. **Internet to Cloudflare Workers.** `/token` and `/github/webhooks` are unauthenticated network endpoints. Form fields, request bodies, addresses/forwarding headers, webhook headers, malformed JWTs, and arbitrary JWT claims are attacker-controlled.
+2. **Subject-token issuer to cyspbot.** GitHub Actions, Fly Machine, and Google service-account tokens carry different claim semantics. Issuer selection must use a configured allowlist before any JWKS network call; `iss`, `aud`, `azp`, subject-token type, signing key, temporal claims, and provider-specific claims are untrusted until the relevant adapter and signature verification accept them.
+3. **Verified claims to authorization policy.** A valid OIDC token only authenticates a workload. CEL policy conditions are code-controlled authorization rules, but their evaluated inputs are claims from an external issuer and the normalized request. The policy must fail closed for absent, mistyped, or surprising claims.
+4. **cyspbot to GitHub.** The Worker signs an App JWT with the private key, resolves the target repository's installation, and requests an installation token. GitHub is the authoritative control plane for the app installation and granted permissions; its HTTP replies and availability are outside the Worker’s control.
+5. **Operators and deployment.** Cloudflare bindings such as `GITHUB_APP_PRIVATE_KEY`, `GITHUB_APP_ID`, `GITHUB_WEBHOOK_SECRET`, rate limiter, and Fly organization configuration are trusted only when deployment configuration is correctly controlled. Source policy, issuer packages, and CI changes are privileged changes because they can expand token-minting authority.
+
+### Assumptions and residual risks
+
+- A configured OIDC issuer signs correct identity claims and protects its signing keys. cyspbot cannot distinguish a legitimate token from a token minted after an upstream issuer compromise.
+- The GitHub App installation is constrained to intended repositories and permissions. A broader installation is still protected by policy only if the checked-in policy remains strict.
+- The token endpoint has no OAuth client authentication by design. Possession of an acceptable subject token is the caller credential, so token theft/replay within its short validity period remains a stated residual risk; the service does not maintain durable `jti` replay state.
+- Cloudflare secret isolation, Worker egress, rate limiting, and GitHub's API TLS authentication are operating assumptions. The code does not itself provide durable audit storage, incident response, or per-subject-token revocation.
+- Operator-controlled repository configuration and policy changes are trusted. A malicious committer or deployment principal can create an allow rule or change a trusted issuer and is therefore in the privileged threat model, not an ordinary remote attacker.
+
+## Attack Surface, Mitigations, and Attacker Stories
+
+### Token exchange
+
+`workers/cyspbot-token-exchange/src/token-exchange.ts` parses bounded form data, accepts only the token-exchange grant and two explicit subject-token type URNs, rejects OAuth client authentication parameters and `Authorization`, requires a specific issued-token type, rejects multiple nonempty parameters, canonicalizes the GitHub API resource, and uses a closed list of GitHub permission scopes. It rate-limits before validation and sends `Cache-Control: no-store` / `Pragma: no-cache` on both success and error responses.
+
+The principal attacker story is a remote workload or internet client attempting to convert a token it legitimately possesses—or an invented/malformed token—into a more privileged GitHub token. Relevant attack classes are signature/issuer confusion, audience or authorized-party confusion, claim confusion across providers, policy-bypass via CEL evaluation or resource canonicalization, duplicate/empty parameter ambiguity, scope expansion, and token leakage through responses, logs, caches, or errors. The service counters these with fixed issuer/JWKS configuration (`packages/oidc/src/verifier.ts` and issuer adapters), explicit algorithm allowlists, exact service audience checks, issuer-specific bindings, fixed policy rule data, exact resource/permission equality, body-size caps, and fail-closed policy evaluation.
+
+The referrer/IP-derived rate-limit key is attacker-influenced and should be regarded as an availability control, not identity. Subject tokens and sensitive request data must remain absent from logs. Replaying a valid subject token before expiry is possible unless the upstream provider or a future service-side anti-replay mechanism prevents it.
+
+### OIDC and outbound JWKS retrieval
+
+The verifier only creates a JWKS client after parsing the unverified issuer and matching it to a trusted issuer configured by an adapter. This makes the JWKS URL service-owned and avoids issuer-driven SSRF. It allowlists signing algorithms and uses `jose` claim validation for issuer/temporal validity, then delegates `aud`, `azp`, subject-token type, and provider claim invariants to the adapter. Provider JWKS failures are separated from invalid assertions to avoid treating provider outages as authentication success.
+
+The most important design risk is semantic rather than cryptographic: each provider adapter needs to validate every claim that makes the issued identity suitable for policy. A generic adapter that accepts a signed but under-bound token can turn an allow rule into a privilege-escalation path. The policy must also account for issuer claim formats, immutable IDs versus names, and whether a provider can mint tokens for unexpected workloads.
+
+### GitHub App issuance
+
+`packages/github/src/app.ts` imports the private key as RS256, creates a short-lived App JWT, resolves the requested repository's installation, and asks GitHub for a token narrowed to the requested repository and policy-approved permissions. The request path is built from parsed owner/repository values, not raw caller URLs. This protects against arbitrary GitHub API targeting if validation remains correct. The failure modes to watch are installation confusion, policy-to-GitHub resource mismatch, credential error handling that hides operator misconfiguration, and leaked installation access tokens in logs or tests.
+
+### Webhook receiver
+
+`workers/cyspbot-github-webhook-receiver/src/github-webhooks/acceptance.ts` limits body size, requires JSON, checks required event/delivery headers and the configured app target, verifies an HMAC-SHA-256 over the raw bytes with a constant-time comparison, then parses JSON. It currently has no business-action sink, lowering impact from malformed but valid payloads. If downstream logic is added, it must preserve signature-first parsing, add idempotency keyed by the delivery ID, constrain event-specific authorization, and avoid SSRF or command execution from payload fields.
+
+### Supply chain, build, and operator paths
+
+Workspace dependencies such as `jose`, CEL, Worker runtime bindings, and GitHub/Cloudflare tooling are part of the trusted computing base. A dependency compromise or unsafe CI/deploy workflow could change the verification or policy behavior. Tests and documentation are not runtime authority but are security-relevant evidence; generated bindings and deployment templates must not accidentally introduce secrets or unaudited production configuration.
+
+## Severity Calibration
+
+### Critical
+
+A remotely exploitable path that mints a GitHub installation token for an unauthorized repository or permission, exposes the GitHub App private key, or permits arbitrary signing-key/JWKS retrieval is Critical. Examples include accepting an attacker-selected issuer/JWKS, bypassing signature/audience/policy validation, or requesting a token broader than the authorized repository/permissions.
+
+### High
+
+A flaw that lets a valid but wrong workload (for example, a different GitHub workflow, Fly organization/machine, or Google service account) satisfy a privileged policy rule is High, as is replay that reliably extends token authority beyond an intended execution context. An HMAC bypass that becomes actionable after webhook downstream effects exist would also be High.
+
+### Medium
+
+Availability attacks that bypass or concentrate rate limits, error paths that materially disclose policy or infrastructure details, weak policy validation that is safely blocked by GitHub's upper authority, or an idempotency gap in a newly stateful webhook consumer are Medium. A provider-specific configuration footgun that fails closed is generally Medium or lower depending on operational impact.
+
+### Low
+
+Minor contract deviations, coarse diagnostics, or local developer-tooling issues without a path to secrets or issued tokens are Low. A signed webhook replay against the current acknowledgement-only handler is Low because it produces no application state change; its severity must be reassessed if event processing is added.
+
+Repository: sha256:6650a4224223ae136af9791bd83f8bc17251e74545253fa0639df4b9b2987f85
+Version: eb7e37e9240c385b9061f0f4a59a91dd8f68d287
*** End Patch";
const r = await tools.apply_patch(patch);
text(typeof r === "string" ? r : JSON.stringify(r));

const r = await tools.exec_command({"cmd":"scan_dir=\"${TMPDIR%/}/codex-security-scans/cyspbot/eb7e37e9240c385b9061f0f4a59a91dd8f68d287_20260710T000000Z\"; cp \"${TMPDIR%/}/codex-security-scans/cyspbot/threat_model.md\" \"$scan_dir/artifacts/01_context/threat_model.md\"; tail -4 \"$scan_dir/artifacts/01_context/threat_model.md\"","workdir":"/Users/cysp/src/cyspbot","yield_time_ms":10000,"max_output_tokens":12000});
text(r.output);

content.text: The architecture is now mapped: this branch replaces a GitHub-Actions-specific principal model with issuer adapters plus generic CEL policy evaluation, and adds Fly/Google authentication paths. I’ve completed the repository-level threat model; next I’m reviewing each changed file and its direct authorization sinks.

*** Add File: /var/folders/hs/pt2n3nn16k3935lw_ljnl5t40000gn/T/codex-security-scans/cyspbot/eb7e37e9240c385b9061f0f4a59a91dd8f68d287_20260710T000000Z/artifacts/02_discovery/finding_discovery_report.md
+# Diff finding discovery
+
+Target: `99475317089cc75f1451b1893ea31ccd17284a92...eb7e37e9240c385b9061f0f4a59a91dd8f68d287`
+
+The deterministic diff worklist contained 24 source-like files. All 24 received a full-file completion receipt in `work_ledger.jsonl`; the ledger paths exactly reconcile to `deep_review_input.jsonl`.
+
+The review followed the changed flow from token-exchange parsing through issuer selection, signature verification, provider bindings, CEL token policy, GitHub installation resolution, and token creation. Direct supports reviewed include the OIDC verifier, GitHub App token sink, existing tests, and the removed GitHub Actions principal parser.
+
+No technically plausible, diff-anchored security candidate survived discovery. In particular, the changed code retains a service-owned issuer/JWKS selection, fixed signing-algorithm allowlists, exact service audience matching, provider-specific token binding, canonical repository-resource parsing, exact permission equality, and an explicit policy allow before the GitHub App token sink. The source diff does introduce provider adapters, but it does not add a production Fly or Google allow rule; authenticated tokens from those issuers are denied unless a later checked-in policy rule authorizes a matching request.
+
+No candidate ledgers, raw candidate records, or dedupe records were created because there were no candidates.
*** End Patch";
const r = await tools.apply_patch(patch);
text(typeof r === "string" ? r : JSON.stringify(r));

content.text: The diff worklist is reconciled: 24 source files, 24 full-file receipts, and no plausible security candidate to carry into validation. The current test environment is stale relative to the branch (new workspace packages are absent), so its full suite cannot start; I’m recording that separately from the source review rather than treating it as a test verdict.

const patch = "*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /var/folders/hs/pt2n3nn16k3935lw_ljnl5t40000gn/T/codex-security-scans/cyspbot/eb7e37e9240c385b9061f0f4a59a91dd8f68d287_20260710T000000Z/artifacts/01_context/oauth_specification_review.md
+# OAuth and OIDC design review
+
+## RFC 8693 token-exchange profile
+
+`/token` uses the RFC 8693 extension grant with `POST` and a form-encoded body. It requires the registered grant URI, a subject token, a subject-token type, and one service-specific `requested_token_type`. RFC 8693 permits additional token-type URIs, so the GitHub installation-token URN is a valid profile choice. It also makes `resource`, `audience`, and `scope` optional request parameters; cyspbot deliberately narrows that flexibility to one canonical GitHub repository resource, one closed permission-scope vocabulary, and no `audience` parameter. Requiring a resource and rejecting multiple resources/audiences is a stricter deployment profile, not a protocol violation.
+
+The success response has the RFC 8693/OAuth shape: `access_token`, `issued_token_type`, `token_type`, `expires_in`, and canonical `scope`, with `Cache-Control: no-store` and `Pragma: no-cache`. Policy denial is returned as `invalid_target`, which RFC 8693 recommends when an authorization server will not issue a token for a requested target. Invalid assertions return a non-descriptive `invalid_request`; the RFC leaves assertion-validity criteria to the token type and allows other suitable OAuth errors.
+
+RFC 8693 makes client authentication a deployment choice, while warning that an unauthenticated STS lets anyone holding a compromised subject token exchange it. cyspbot intentionally chooses bearer subject-token authentication and rejects OAuth client credentials rather than silently ignoring them. That is coherent with the workload-identity design, but it means subject-token theft/replay before expiry is a residual risk rather than an additional client-bound check.
+
+Sources:
+
+- https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8693.html
+- https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9700.html
+
+## OIDC issuer and claim validation
+
+The code first reads an unverified `iss` solely to choose a preconfigured adapter, then calls `jose.jwtVerify` with the selected fixed issuer, fixed JWKS URI, and RS256 allowlist. It performs a separate exact string audience check for `cyspbot`, and each adapter applies its provider binding before CEL authorization. This preserves the key OIDC invariants: issuer, signature, expiration, intended audience, and an authorized-party policy.
+
+OpenID Connect Core permits an audience array provided the relying party is an allowed audience, and recommends checking `azp` when it is present. cyspbot rejects multi-valued audiences and, for GitHub/Fly, only permits absent `azp` or `azp=cyspbot`; those are stricter compatibility choices that reduce ambiguity. Google service-account tokens are checked with `azp === sub`, matching Google's published service-account ID-token claim semantics. The generic verifier relies on `jose` for expiry validation and adapters require `exp`; it does not separately require `iat` for GitHub or Google, which is a standards-profile gap with no demonstrated authorization bypass because issuer/signature/audience/expiry/policy still gate issuance.
+
+Sources:
+
+- https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0-18.html
+- https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/token-types
+- https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/security/oidc
+
+## Provider-specific authorization semantics
+
+GitHub Actions' current documentation supports both legacy name-based and immutable owner/repository-ID subject forms. The migrated production CEL rules retain checks for repository, event, branch ref, workflow ref, and either supported `sub` shape. The exact issuer/JWKS selection is service-owned, so an attacker cannot make the Worker fetch a JWKS based only on an arbitrary JWT issuer.
+
+Fly documents an organization-specific issuer and short-lived Machine tokens whose claims include app, machine, and organization identifiers. cyspbot's configured organization slugs become fixed issuer/JWKS pairs; the adapter only authenticates `sub`, `exp`, `nbf`, audience, and optional `azp`, leaving app/machine authorization to an explicit CEL rule. That separation is sound only when Fly policy rules bind immutable `org_id`, `app_id`, or `machine_id` (names alone are a documented compatibility option). No production Fly rule exists in this branch, so authentication alone cannot mint a token.
+
+Google service-account tokens are similarly authentication-capable but not authorized by the production rule set. Any future email-based CEL rule should require `email_verified == true` as the implementation guide says; a safer primary identity is the immutable `sub` service-account ID.
+
+Sources:
+
+- https://fly.io/docs/security/openid-connect/
+- https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/token-types
+
+## GitHub token-minting sink
+
+The endpoint parses one canonical GitHub API resource, checks exact policy resource and permissions before contacting GitHub, resolves that repository's installation, and asks GitHub for a token constrained to `repositories: [repository]` and the requested permissions. GitHub documents both parameters and its one-hour installation-token lifetime. This is a sound defence-in-depth relationship: the policy supplies a lower bound, while the GitHub App installation supplies the upper bound.
+
+The remaining token-use risk is bearer-token replay. RFC 9700 recommends sender-constraining access tokens where possible; GitHub installation tokens are bearer credentials and this service has no DPoP/mTLS capability at the GitHub API. Limit policy grants, request only explicit minimal scopes, protect responses/logs, and revoke installation tokens during incident response.
+
+Source:
+
+- https://docs.github.com/en/rest/apps/apps#create-an-installation-access-token-for-an-app
*** Add File: /var/folders/hs/pt2n3nn16k3935lw_ljnl5t40000gn/T/codex-security-scans/cyspbot/eb7e37e9240c385b9061f0f4a59a91dd8f68d287_20260710T000000Z/artifacts/03_coverage/reviewed_surfaces.md
+# Reviewed surfaces
+
+| Surface | Risk area | Outcome | Notes |
+|---|---|---|---|
+| Issuer adapters and OIDC verification | Issuer confusion, attacker-selected JWKS, audience and authorized-party validation | No issue found | Issuer selection is configuration-owned; adapters select fixed issuer/JWKS material before signature verification and policy authorization. |
+| Generic CEL token policy and migrated GitHub Actions rules | Authorization bypass and claim confusion | No issue found | Exact issuer, resource, permissions, and a boolean CEL condition must match. GitHub rules retain repository, event, branch/ref, subject, and workflow-ref checks. |
+| Token-exchange endpoint and runtime composition | Parameter ambiguity, target/scope escalation, rate-limit or binding bypass | No issue found | Single-value parsing, canonical resource and closed scope normalization occur before authentication; runtime composition does not bypass policy or GitHub issuance. |
+| GitHub App issuance integration | Confused deputy and over-broad installation token | No issue found | Policy is evaluated before installation lookup; issuance is constrained by a parsed repository and exact permissions, with GitHub App installation authority as an upper bound. |
+
+The 24 source-like files generated from the branch diff each have a full-file receipt in `../02_discovery/work_ledger.jsonl`. Changed tests and contract/documentation were read as supporting evidence for the design review; the local full suite could not load the new workspace packages because `node_modules` predates this branch.

content.text:
No reportable, diff-anchored security vulnerability survived review. The sealed report is [here](/private/var/folders/hs/pt2n3nn16k3935lw_ljnl5t40000gn/T/codex-security-scans/cyspbot/eb7e37e9240c385b9061f0f4a59a91dd8f68d287_20260710T000000Z/report.md).

Compared with current `origin/main`, this branch replaces the GitHub-Actions-specific principal with fixed issuer adapters and CEL policy conditions, adds Fly/Google authentication, and makes `resource` explicit for every caller. The issuer/JWKS choice remains service-owned; exact audience, provider binding, normalized target/permissions, policy allow, and GitHub’s installation constraints all gate token minting.

OAuth/OIDC assessment:

- The RFC 8693 endpoint profile is sound: form POST, explicit grant/token types, canonical resource/scopes, OAuth-shaped responses, and `invalid_target` for policy/target denial. Requiring exactly one resource and rejecting `audience` is a deliberately stricter profile than the RFC permits. [RFC 8693](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8693.html)
- OIDC verification is robust against issuer/JWKS confusion. It intentionally rejects multi-valued `aud`, stricter than OIDC Core’s permitted audience arrays. [OpenID Connect Core](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0-18.html)
- Google’s `azp === sub` check matches published service-account ID-token semantics. [Google token types](https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/token-types)
- Fly authentication alone cannot mint tokens: production policy currently has no Fly or Google allow rule. That is deny-by-default and safe, but README wording should clarify “can authenticate” versus “can exchange when policy grants.” Fly recommends binding app/machine identity claims, which future CEL rules should do with immutable IDs. [Fly OIDC docs](https://fly.io/docs/security/openid-connect/)
- GitHub issuance is narrowed to a repository plus exact permissions; GitHub treats the App installation as the upper authority. [GitHub App API](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/apps/apps#create-an-installation-access-token-for-an-app)

Residual adversarial risk, not a new finding: this is intentionally a bearer-token STS—no client authentication or durable `jti` replay prevention. A stolen valid subject token can be exchanged before expiry, and GitHub installation tokens remain bearer credentials. RFC 8693 explicitly calls out this tradeoff; RFC 9700 recommends sender-constraining tokens where supported. [RFC 8693](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8693.html), [RFC 9700](https://www.rfc-editor.org/info/rfc9700/)

Validation: `git diff --check` passed. The Node 24 test run could not start tests because this checkout’s `node_modules` predates the branch and lacks new workspace packages and `@bufbuild/cel`; this is not a behavioral failure. The worktree remains unchanged except for pre-existing untracked `.pnpm-store/`.

Scan goal completed in ~13 minutes (228,364 tokens).
